q: Who is accountable for managing the Product Backlog?
options: The Scrum Master
The Product Owner
The Developers
The Scrum Team collectively
why: The Product Owner is accountable for effective Product Backlog management — developing and communicating the Product Goal, creating and ordering Product Backlog items, and ensuring the backlog is transparent, visible, and understood.

q: Who has the authority to cancel a Sprint?
options: The Scrum Master
Only the Product Owner
The Developers by consensus
Management or stakeholders
why: Only the Product Owner has the authority to cancel a Sprint. A Sprint would be cancelled if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete.

q: Who manages the Sprint Backlog?
options: The Product Owner
The Scrum Master
The Developers
The Scrum Team collectively
why: The Sprint Backlog is a plan by and for the Developers. They manage it and update it throughout the Sprint as more is learned.

q: The Product Owner accountability can be shared among multiple people acting as a committee.
options: True
False
why: False. The Product Owner is one person, not a committee. They may represent the needs of many stakeholders, but accountability cannot be divided.

q: The Scrum Master is best described as:
options: A project manager who plans the work
A true leader who serves the Scrum Team and organization
A task assigner for the Developers
A neutral facilitator with no real accountability
why: The Scrum Master is a true leader who serves the Scrum Team, the Product Owner, and the organization. The Scrum Master is accountable for the Scrum Team's effectiveness — not a project manager, not a task-assigner.

q: Who is responsible for monitoring progress toward the Sprint Goal?
options: The Scrum Master
The Product Owner
The Developers
A delivery manager
why: The Developers inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal at least daily (typically in the Daily Scrum) and adjust the Sprint Backlog as needed.

q: What is the maximum duration of a Sprint?
options: One week
Two weeks
One month
Three months
why: Sprints are fixed-length events of one month or less to create consistency. A new Sprint starts immediately after the previous one concludes.

q: The Daily Scrum is time-boxed to:
options: 15 minutes
30 minutes
As long as the Developers need
1 hour
why: The Daily Scrum is a 15-minute event for the Developers. Holding it at the same time and place each day reduces complexity.

q: For a one-month Sprint, Sprint Planning is time-boxed to a maximum of:
options: 2 hours
4 hours
8 hours
No explicit limit
why: Sprint Planning is time-boxed to a maximum of eight hours for a one-month Sprint. For shorter Sprints, the event is usually shorter.

q: For a one-month Sprint, the Sprint Review is time-boxed to a maximum of:
options: 2 hours
4 hours
6 hours
8 hours
why: Sprint Review is time-boxed to a maximum of four hours for a one-month Sprint. Shorter Sprints have shorter Reviews.

q: For a one-month Sprint, the Sprint Retrospective is time-boxed to a maximum of:
options: 1 hour
2 hours
3 hours
4 hours
why: The Sprint Retrospective is time-boxed to a maximum of three hours for a one-month Sprint.

q: Who must attend the Daily Scrum?
options: The entire Scrum Team plus all stakeholders
The Developers. The Product Owner and Scrum Master attend if actively working on items
Only the Scrum Master and Developers
Only the Product Owner and Developers
why: The Daily Scrum is for the Developers. The Product Owner or Scrum Master participate as Developers only if they are actively working on items in the Sprint Backlog.

q: The Sprint Review is a status meeting where the Scrum Team reports progress to management.
options: True
False
why: False. The Sprint Review is a working session to inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog based on collaboration between the Scrum Team and stakeholders. It is not a status-report meeting.

q: When does the next Sprint start?
options: After the Product Owner formally approves the Increment
Immediately after the conclusion of the previous Sprint
After a release planning meeting
Whenever the Developers are ready
why: A new Sprint starts immediately after the conclusion of the previous Sprint. There is no gap.

q: The primary purpose of the Sprint Retrospective is to:
options: Demonstrating the Increment to stakeholders
Planning the next Sprint
Inspecting how the Sprint went and identifying improvements
Allowing the Scrum Master to give feedback to individuals
why: The Sprint Retrospective plans ways to increase quality and effectiveness. The Scrum Team inspects how the last Sprint went regarding individuals, interactions, processes, tools, and the Definition of Done, then identifies high-priority improvements.

q: Which of the following are formal events in Scrum? (Select all that apply)
options: The Sprint
Sprint Planning
Daily Scrum
Backlog Refinement
Sprint Review
Sprint Retrospective
Release Planning
why: There are exactly five events: the Sprint (a container for the others), Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, and Sprint Retrospective. Backlog Refinement is an ongoing activity, not a formal event. Release Planning is not part of Scrum.

q: When is a Product Backlog item considered 'Done'?
options: When the Developers say it's done
When the Product Owner accepts it in the Sprint Review
When it meets the Definition of Done
When it is deployed to production
why: A Product Backlog item is Done when it meets the Definition of Done. The moment it meets DoD, it becomes part of the Increment.

q: The Sprint Goal is:
options: A list of all work selected for the Sprint
The single objective for the Sprint
A forecast of future Sprints
A contract with stakeholders
why: The Sprint Goal is the single objective for the Sprint. It is the commitment for the Sprint Backlog and creates coherence and focus for the Developers.

q: The Product Goal is best described as:
options: A deadline for delivery
The long-term objective for the Scrum Team — a future state of the product
The same thing as the Sprint Goal
A binding contract with customers
why: The Product Goal describes a future state of the product. It is the long-term objective for the Scrum Team and serves as the commitment for the Product Backlog.

q: The Product Backlog is an ordered list.
options: True
False
why: True. The Product Backlog is an emergent, ordered list of what is needed to improve the product. Ordering is a responsibility of the Product Owner.

q: An Increment is:
options: All work completed in the Sprint, including unfinished items
A concrete stepping stone toward the Product Goal that is Done and usable
A release to production customers
The set of user stories selected for the Sprint
why: An Increment is a concrete stepping stone toward the Product Goal. Each Increment is additive to all prior Increments and thoroughly verified. It must meet the Definition of Done to be presented at the Sprint Review.

q: Which artifact is paired with the correct commitment? (Select all that apply)
options: Product Backlog → Product Goal
Sprint Backlog → Sprint Goal
Increment → Definition of Done
Product Backlog → Sprint Goal
Increment → Product Goal
why: The three commitments are: Product Goal (for the Product Backlog), Sprint Goal (for the Sprint Backlog), and Definition of Done (for the Increment).

q: Who creates the Definition of Done?
options: The Product Owner establishes it
The Scrum Master creates it for the team
If an organization standard exists, the team follows it as a minimum; otherwise the team creates its own
The stakeholders determine it collectively
why: If the organization has a Definition of Done as a standard, all Scrum Teams must follow it as a minimum. If no organizational standard exists, the Scrum Team must create one appropriate for the product.

q: According to the Scrum Guide, a Scrum Team is typically:
options: 7 ± 2 people
10 or fewer people
Exactly 9 people
As many as needed
why: The Scrum Guide says Scrum Teams are typically 10 or fewer people. Smaller teams communicate better and are more productive; if they get too large, they should consider reorganizing into multiple cohesive Scrum Teams.

q: What does 'self-managing' mean for a Scrum Team?
options: They have no manager and answer to nobody
They internally decide who does what, when, and how
They don't need a Scrum Master
Each member manages their own work in isolation
why: Self-managing Scrum Teams internally decide who does what, when, and how. They are also cross-functional, meaning members have all the skills necessary to create value each Sprint.

q: When multiple Scrum Teams work together on the same product, they share:
options: Only the Scrum Master, but each team has its own PO and backlog
The Product Owner, the Product Backlog, and the Product Goal
The Definition of Done only, with separate PO per team
Nothing — each team operates independently
why: If multiple Scrum Teams work together on a product, they share the same Product Goal, Product Backlog, and Product Owner — to maintain a single source of direction and value.

q: Which of the following are the five Scrum Values? (Select all that apply)
options: Commitment
Focus
Trust
Openness
Respect
Accountability
Courage
why: The five Scrum Values are Commitment, Focus, Openness, Respect, and Courage. Trust is a result of living the values, not one of them. Accountability is a concept in Scrum but not one of the five values.

q: Which are the three pillars of empiricism in Scrum? (Select all that apply)
options: Transparency
Inspection
Adaptation
Commitment
Communication
why: Empiricism rests on three pillars: Transparency (the process must be visible), Inspection (artifacts and progress are frequently inspected), and Adaptation (adjustments are made when deviations are detected).

q: During the Sprint, the Developers realize they have selected more work than they can finish. What should they do?
options: Extend the Sprint to finish the work
Work overtime to complete everything
Work with the Product Owner to renegotiate scope within the Sprint Goal
Cancel the Sprint
why: As more is learned, scope may be clarified and renegotiated with the Product Owner — without compromising the Sprint Goal. Sprints are not extended; the Sprint Backlog is adjusted.

q: If the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete during the Sprint:
options: The Developers continue with the original plan
The Scrum Master must extend the Sprint
The Product Owner may cancel the Sprint
The Scrum Team must escalate to management
why: If the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete, the Sprint may be cancelled. Only the Product Owner has the authority to cancel a Sprint.

q: Scrum requires which document for capturing product requirements?
options: User stories
Use cases
A formal specification
No specific format is mandated
why: Scrum does not prescribe a format for Product Backlog items. User stories are a common practice but are not required by Scrum.

q: Product Backlog refinement is a formal Scrum event with a mandatory time-box.
options: True
False
why: False. Refinement is an ongoing activity to add detail, estimates, and order to Product Backlog items. It is not one of the five formal events.

q: Who can change the Sprint Backlog during the Sprint?
options: Only the Product Owner
Only the Scrum Master
The Developers
Any stakeholder with a legitimate need
why: The Sprint Backlog is owned by the Developers. They update it throughout the Sprint as they learn more and progress toward the Sprint Goal.

q: A Scrum Master is essentially a renamed project manager.
options: True
False
why: False. A Scrum Master is a true leader who serves the Scrum Team and organization. They do not manage people, assign tasks, or own delivery commitments the way a traditional project manager would.

q: A stakeholder asks the Developers to add a new feature mid-Sprint. What is the most appropriate response?
options: The Developers add it immediately to be responsive
The Scrum Master adds it to the Sprint Backlog
The request is directed to the Product Owner, who may add it to the Product Backlog for consideration in a future Sprint
The Sprint is cancelled so the work can be replanned
why: The Product Owner is the single source of ordering for the Product Backlog. New requests go to the Product Owner. Only the Product Owner can renegotiate Sprint scope, and only in a way that doesn't endanger the Sprint Goal.

q: The Scrum Team is cross-functional. This primarily means:
options: Every Developer can do every task equally well
The team has all the skills to create value each Sprint without outside help
The team rotates roles every Sprint
The team reports to multiple functional managers
why: Cross-functional means the Scrum Team collectively has all the skills required to create value each Sprint. It does not mean every individual has every skill.

q: The Definition of Done can evolve over time as the Scrum Team matures and capabilities improve.
options: True
False
why: True. The Scrum Guide 2020 states that as Scrum Teams mature, their Definitions of Done are expected to expand to include more stringent criteria for higher quality.

q: The purpose of a Sprint is to:
options: Maximize Developer productivity
Complete all planned Product Backlog items
Meet a release deadline
Turn ideas into value through a Done Increment
why: Sprints exist to create a Done Increment that delivers value — not to maximize activity, tick off planned items, or hit a fixed release date. The purpose is outcome-focused: usable working product that advances the Product Goal at a sustainable cadence.

q: In what way does the Scrum Master serve the Product Owner?
options: By managing the Product Backlog directly
By helping find techniques for effective Product Goal definition and backlog management
By writing user stories on behalf of the PO
By deciding release dates
why: The Scrum Master helps the Scrum Team — including the PO — find effective practices: defining the Product Goal, managing the Product Backlog, and empirical planning in a complex environment.

q: In what way does the Scrum Master serve the Developers?
options: By assigning tasks to Developers
By reporting their progress to management
By coaching them in self-management and removing impediments
By choosing which Product Backlog items they work on
why: The SM coaches the Developers in self-management and cross-functionality, helps them focus on creating high-value Increments that meet the DoD, and removes impediments to their progress.

q: How does the Scrum Master serve the organization?
options: By tracking team velocity for executive dashboards
By leading, training, and coaching the organization in Scrum adoption and planning implementations
By setting release dates with the business
By reporting risks and dependencies to project managers
why: The SM serves the organization by leading, training, and coaching its Scrum adoption, planning and advising implementations, and helping employees and stakeholders understand an empirical approach.

q: Who is accountable for establishing Scrum as defined in the Scrum Guide?
options: The Product Owner
The Scrum Master
The Developers
The organization's leadership
why: The Scrum Master is accountable for establishing Scrum as defined in the Scrum Guide. They do this by helping everyone understand Scrum theory and practice.

q: During Sprint Planning, who proposes how the product could increase its value in the upcoming Sprint?
options: The Scrum Master
The Product Owner
The Developers
The stakeholders
why: In Sprint Planning Topic One ('Why is this Sprint valuable?'), the Product Owner proposes how the product could increase its value. The whole Scrum Team then collaborates to define a Sprint Goal.

q: During Sprint Planning, who decides how many Product Backlog items to include in the Sprint?
options: The Product Owner, based on priorities
The Scrum Master, based on past velocity
The Developers, forecasting their capacity in collaboration with the Product Owner
Stakeholders, during Sprint Planning
why: The Developers forecast what they can accomplish during the Sprint. They discuss with the PO to make selections, but the capacity call belongs to the Developers.

q: Which of the following are true about the Sprint? (Select all that apply)
options: A new Sprint starts immediately after the previous one concludes
A Sprint may be extended if the Developers need more time
All other Scrum events occur within the Sprint
Sprints are fixed length of one month or less
The Sprint can be cancelled if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete
why: Sprints are fixed-length (one month max) and cannot be extended. They may be cancelled (only by the PO) if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete. All other events happen within the Sprint.

q: The Daily Scrum improves communications, identifies impediments, and reduces the need for other meetings.
options: True
False
why: True. Per the Scrum Guide, the Daily Scrum improves communications, identifies impediments, promotes quick decision-making, and consequently reduces the need for other meetings.

q: The Sprint Retrospective is attended by:
options: The Developers only
The Product Owner only
The entire Scrum Team
The Scrum Team and key stakeholders
why: The Sprint Retrospective is for the whole Scrum Team — Developers, Product Owner, and Scrum Master — to inspect itself and identify improvements.

q: For Sprints shorter than one month, the other Scrum events are usually proportionally shorter.
options: True
False
why: True. Event time-boxes scale down for shorter Sprints. For example, Sprint Planning maxes at eight hours for a one-month Sprint and is usually shorter for shorter Sprints.

q: The Sprint Backlog is composed of:
options: Only the selected Product Backlog items
The Sprint Goal (why), selected items (what), and an actionable delivery plan (how)
A list of daily tasks assigned
A Gantt chart of the Sprint timeline
why: The Sprint Backlog has three parts: Sprint Goal (why), selected PBIs (what), and an actionable plan for delivery (how). It makes visible the work needed to achieve the Sprint Goal.

q: What is the primary purpose of the Definition of Done?
options: List acceptance criteria for one item
Give everyone a shared understanding of what 'Done' means for the Increment
Mark when the Sprint ends
Enforce coding standards across teams
why: The Definition of Done creates transparency by giving everyone a shared understanding of what work was completed as part of the Increment. It's the commitment for the Increment.

q: Multiple Increments can be created within a single Sprint.
options: True
False
why: True. An Increment is created whenever a Product Backlog item meets the Definition of Done. Multiple Increments may be created within a Sprint, each usable as soon as Done.

q: Which artifact-to-commitment pairing is correct?
options: Product Backlog → Definition of Done
Sprint Backlog → Product Goal
Increment → Sprint Goal
Sprint Backlog → Sprint Goal
why: The three pairings: Product Backlog → Product Goal, Sprint Backlog → Sprint Goal, Increment → Definition of Done.

q: Product Backlog items should be broken down so they can be Done within a single Sprint.
options: True
False
why: True. Items that can be Done by the Scrum Team within one Sprint are deemed ready for selection in Sprint Planning. Appropriate sizing is part of refinement.

q: What are the Developers accountable for?
options: Writing and testing code exclusively
Creating the Sprint Backlog plan, ensuring quality through DoD, daily adaptation, and mutual accountability
Managing the Product Backlog and prioritizing items
Reporting progress to the Scrum Master daily
why: The Scrum Guide 2020 lists these as the Developers' accountabilities. 'Developers' refers to anyone creating the Increment — not just programmers.

q: A single person can hold both the Scrum Master and the Developer accountabilities on the same team.
options: True
False
why: True. The accountabilities are distinct but can be held by the same person. The Scrum Master participates as a Developer when actively working on Sprint Backlog items.

q: What does 'cross-functional' mean for a Scrum Team?
options: Every member has every skill needed
Members can rotate through departments quarterly
The team collectively has all skills to create value each Sprint without outside help
The team reports to multiple functional managers
why: Cross-functional means the team collectively possesses all the skills required to create value each Sprint. Individual specialization is fine; collective capability is what matters.

q: Which statements about empiricism in Scrum are true? (Select all that apply)
options: Empiricism means making decisions based on what is observed
Empiricism requires detailed upfront planning
Empiricism rests on Transparency, Inspection, and Adaptation
Empiricism is optional in Scrum
Empiricism helps the Scrum Team deal with complexity
why: Empiricism asserts that knowledge comes from experience and decisions are based on what is observed. Its three pillars are Transparency, Inspection, and Adaptation. It is foundational — not optional.

q: If an artifact's transparency is incomplete, what is the risk?
options: The team will be more efficient
Decisions based on distorted information may be flawed and increase risk
The Sprint will automatically fail
There is no risk
why: Without transparency, inspection is misleading and adaptation is misdirected. Decisions based on distorted artifacts can reduce value and increase risk.

q: The Scrum Values (Commitment, Focus, Openness, Respect, Courage) are optional guidelines.
options: True
False
why: False. The Scrum Values are integral to Scrum. When embodied by the Scrum Team, the pillars of empiricism come to life and trust is built.

q: When should a Scrum Team adapt?
options: Only at the Sprint Retrospective
As soon as inspection detects deviations outside acceptable limits
Only when management approves
Never mid-Sprint
why: Adaptation must be done as soon as possible to minimize further deviation. The Scrum Team does not wait for a formal event to adapt if inspection reveals a problem.

q: The Developers are frequently interrupted with urgent requests from outside the team. What should the Scrum Master do?
options: Instruct the stakeholders to stop interrupting
Coach both sides to protect focus, route requests through the Product Owner, and address root causes
Reassign the requests to other teams without discussion
Ignore it — it's not the SM's concern
why: The Scrum Master serves the team and the organization. Chronic interruptions are a systemic impediment. The SM coaches both sides and works on the underlying causes.

q: A Developer says the Sprint Goal cannot be achieved. What should the Scrum Master do?
options: Extend the Sprint by a few days
Cancel the Sprint immediately
Facilitate conversation to inspect and adapt the plan toward the Sprint Goal
Reassign the work to another Developer
why: The SM facilitates empiricism: inspect, then adapt. Scope can be renegotiated with the PO. Cancellation is only if the Sprint Goal itself becomes obsolete, and only by the PO.

q: The organization asks the Scrum Team to skip the Sprint Retrospective to save time. The Scrum Master should:
options: Comply — saving time is a legitimate organizational concern
Coach the organization on why the Retrospective drives empirical improvement, and protect the event
Combine the Retrospective with the Sprint Review to satisfy both ends
Run a shorter Retrospective privately with just the Developers
why: Events are not optional. Skipping the Retrospective removes the formal opportunity to inspect and adapt how the team works — undermining continuous improvement.

q: Developers disagree on how to implement a Product Backlog item. What should happen?
options: The Scrum Master decides the technical approach
The Product Owner breaks the tie based on business value
The Developers, as self-managing professionals, resolve it together
Escalate to a senior architect outside the team
why: Self-managing teams decide who does what, when, and how. Internal technical disagreements are for the Developers to resolve together.

q: Management demands a fixed-scope, fixed-date commitment for the next six months. How should the Scrum Master respond?
options: Make the commitment on behalf of the team
Refuse to engage with management at all
Help management understand empiricism and forecasts that evolve as learning occurs
Tell the Developers to work overtime
why: Complex product development is empirical. Forecasts based on evidence are honest; fixed long-term commitments across scope and date usually break under real-world complexity.

q: During the Daily Scrum, Developers give status reports rather than inspecting progress toward the Sprint Goal. The Scrum Master should:
options: Run the Daily Scrum themselves directly
Cancel the Daily Scrum until focus improves
Coach the Developers on the event's purpose and refocus on progress toward the Sprint Goal
Require written reports instead of meetings
why: The Daily Scrum is for the Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the Sprint Backlog. The SM coaches; they don't run it.

q: The Developers haven't updated the Sprint Backlog in several days. This primarily harms:
options: The Scrum Master's workload
Transparency of progress toward the Sprint Goal
The Product Owner's holiday plans
Nothing — the Sprint Backlog is informal
why: The Sprint Backlog makes progress toward the Sprint Goal transparent. Without updates, inspection is compromised and decisions suffer.

q: A new Developer joins the team mid-Sprint. What is the most Scrum-aligned approach?
options: The Scrum Master assigns the new Developer to specific tasks
The Product Owner reassigns Sprint Backlog items to the new Developer
The Developers, as self-managing, decide together how to integrate the new member
The Sprint is cancelled to avoid mid-Sprint disruption
why: Self-managing Developers decide how to integrate changes in team composition. They adapt the plan as needed to pursue the Sprint Goal.

q: The Scrum Team consistently fails to meet the Definition of Done. The Scrum Master should first:
options: Lower the Definition of Done standards
Replace team members who struggle
Coach the team to understand why the DoD isn't met and facilitate improvements
Escalate to executive leadership
why: The SM coaches the team to diagnose root causes — quality issues, unclear DoD, inadequate skills, environmental constraints — and facilitates improvement. Lowering the DoD creates technical debt.

q: A Scrum Team has used the same Definition of Done for a year without change. This suggests:
options: A mature, stable team with strong performance
A potential issue — teams should expand their DoD as they mature and capabilities improve
Nothing in particular worth noting
The team is violating Scrum fundamentally
why: As Scrum Teams mature, their DoD is expected to expand to include more stringent criteria for higher quality. Stagnation may signal missed improvement opportunities.

q: During the Sprint Retrospective, the team identifies an important improvement. When should it be acted on?
options: At the end of the next release cycle
As soon as possible — often in the very next Sprint
Only if the Product Owner approves in writing
Discussed but rarely acted on in practice
why: The most impactful improvement identified in the Retrospective is addressed as soon as possible — often in the upcoming Sprint's Sprint Backlog.

q: A team wants to adopt Scrum but senior leadership does not understand the framework. The Scrum Master should:
options: Tell the team to abandon Scrum adoption
Lead, train, and coach the organization in Scrum adoption and planning implementations
Wait for a directive from executives
Quietly implement Scrum without leadership awareness or buy-in
why: Serving the organization includes leading and coaching Scrum adoption and helping stakeholders understand empirical product development.

q: What is the Scrum Master's role during Scrum events?
options: To lead and direct all discussions
To ensure events are positive, productive, and kept within the time-box
To make decisions for the team
To take minutes and distribute notes
why: The Scrum Master ensures that all Scrum events take place and are positive, productive, and kept within the time-box. The SM facilitates; the team does the work.

q: Which are true about multiple Scrum Teams working on the same product? (Select all that apply)
options: They share one Product Goal
They share one Product Backlog
They share one Product Owner
Each team has its own Product Backlog
They share a consistent Definition of Done for the integrated Increment
why: Multiple teams on one product share: one Product Goal, one Product Backlog, one Product Owner, and a consistent Definition of Done (organizational standard or one created by the teams together).

q: Which of the following is NOT one of the five formal Scrum events?
options: Sprint Planning
Daily Scrum
Product Backlog Refinement
Sprint Review
why: The five events are Sprint, Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, and Sprint Retrospective. Product Backlog Refinement is an ongoing activity, not a formal event.

q: Scrum is founded on empiricism and lean thinking.
options: True
False
why: True. The Scrum Guide 2020 explicitly states: 'Scrum is founded on empiricism and lean thinking.'

q: If the Product Owner is unavailable for Sprint Planning, what is the most appropriate outcome?
options: Cancel the Sprint
Provide the PO as Product Owner
Spring Planning cannot proceed without the PO; the SM resolves the availability issue
Developers select items
why: The PO is essential to Sprint Planning — proposing value, clarifying PBIs, and shaping the Sprint Goal. The SM addresses the systemic issue that the PO isn't engaged.

q: Two Developers routinely bypass the Definition of Done to deliver faster. The Scrum Master should:
options: Praise them for the speed
Coach the team on quality, transparency, and the DoD
Report them to management
Remove them from the team
why: The SM coaches the team on the value of the DoD. Skipping it creates hidden technical debt and erodes long-term value. The team is accountable for instilling quality.

q: Work that does not meet the Definition of Done can be released as long as the Product Owner approves.
options: True
False
why: False. Work that does not meet the DoD cannot be released or considered part of the Increment. It is returned to the Product Backlog for future consideration.

q: A stakeholder complains directly to the Developers that the product is 'behind schedule.' The most Scrum-aligned response is:
options: The Developers commit to working weekends to catch up
The Developers direct the stakeholder to the Product Owner and coach on empirical engagement
The Scrum Master shields the team by refusing engagement
The Developers change the Sprint Backlog to please the stakeholder
why: Stakeholder engagement on value, scope, and timing goes through the Product Owner. The SM helps both sides understand empirical progress and stakeholder involvement at the Sprint Review.

q: The Product Owner is accountable for developing and communicating what?
options: The Definition of Done
The Product Goal and the Product Backlog
The Sprint plan
The team's velocity metrics
why: The Product Owner is accountable for developing and communicating the Product Goal and for effective Product Backlog management. The other items are not PO accountabilities.

q: The Product Owner is accountable for the technical quality of the product.
options: True
False
why: False. The Developers are accountable for quality through instilling it via the Definition of Done. The Product Owner focuses on value and ordering.

q: A Developer becomes the Scrum Master for the same team. What is primarily at risk?
options: The team's velocity
The Scrum Master's ability to serve as an impartial coach and change agent
The project timeline
The Definition of Done
why: While one person can hold multiple roles, combining Developer and SM creates a conflict of interest. The SM must serve and coach the team objectively, which is harder when also doing development work.

q: The Scrum Master's accountability for team effectiveness means:
options: Assigning work to meet velocity targets
Removing impediments and coaching the team on self-management and Scrum practices
Collecting metrics to report to management
Making architectural decisions
why: Scrum Master effectiveness is about removing impediments, coaching self-management and cross-functionality, and helping the team embrace empiricism.

q: When a critical bug is discovered mid-Sprint, who decides whether to pull it into the current Sprint?
options: The Scrum Master
The Developers and Product Owner together
The Product Owner unilaterally
Management
why: The Product Owner prioritizes and can propose adding it, but the Developers decide what they can fit while protecting the Sprint Goal. It's a collaborative decision.

q: Which of the following is a Scrum Master accountability?
options: Writing user stories
Estimating Product Backlog items
Coaching the organization on Scrum and removing organizational impediments
Signing off on Increments
why: The SM serves the organization by coaching it on Scrum adoption, identifying and helping remove systemic impediments, and advising implementations.

q: If the Developers and Product Owner cannot agree on Sprint scope, the Scrum Master should:
options: Make the decision to break the tie
Facilitate a conversation so both sides understand each other and reach agreement
Let the team default to lower scope
Escalate to management
why: The SM facilitates, not directs. They help the team inspect the situation and adapt collaboratively.

q: The Scrum Master notices that Product Backlog items are unclear and not ordered well. What should the SM do?
options: Reorder the backlog themselves
Coach the Product Owner on effective Product Backlog management
Tell the Developers to ignore unclear items
Cancel the next Sprint
why: The SM serves the PO by coaching on effective practices: clear item description, appropriate ordering, and visibility of the backlog.

q: The Developers are accountable for holding each other accountable as professionals.
options: True
False
why: True. This is explicit in the Scrum Guide. Developers are self-managing and hold themselves and each other accountable for creating quality work.

q: A Scrum Team member lacks the skills needed for a critical Sprint task. Who is primarily accountable for addressing this gap?
options: The Product Owner
The Scrum Master should coach and help remove the impediment
The specific Developer
Management training department
why: The SM removes impediments to the team's effectiveness, including skill gaps. This may mean coaching, arranging pairing, finding resources, or other actions.

q: Sprint Planning Topic One focuses on answering which question?
options: Who will do what?
How will the work be done?
Why is this Sprint valuable?
When will we release?
why: The Scrum Guide identifies three topics in Sprint Planning. Topic One: 'Why is this Sprint valuable?' The PO proposes how the product could increase value.

q: Sprint Planning Topic Two focuses on answering which question?
options: Why is this Sprint valuable?
What can be Done this Sprint and how will the work be done?
When will the work be released?
Who is the Product Owner?
why: Topic Two: 'What can be Done this Sprint and how will the work be done?' The Developers plan the work needed to achieve the Sprint Goal.

q: If a one-week Sprint is used, what is the typical maximum time-box for Sprint Planning?
options: 2 hours
4 hours
8 hours
No limit applies to short Sprints
why: Sprint Planning is time-boxed to eight hours for a one-month Sprint. For shorter Sprints, the event is proportionally shorter. A one-week Sprint would typically have Planning around two hours or less.

q: What is the single most important purpose of the Sprint Review?
options: To collect feedback from the Product Owner
To present a status report to management
To inspect the Increment with stakeholders and adapt the Product Backlog
To celebrate the team's effort
why: The Sprint Review is a working session where the Scrum Team and stakeholders collaborate to inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog based on what was learned.

q: The Sprint Review includes a working demo or presentation of the Increment.
options: True
False
why: True. The Scrum Team presents the work that is Done (the Increment) and discusses what was accomplished and what challenges were encountered.

q: If multiple Scrum Teams are using two-week Sprints, what is the maximum duration for their combined Sprint Reviews?
options: 4 hours total
Each team has a 4-hour review, so 8+ hours combined
2 hours per team; combined depends on number of teams
The Scrum Guide does not address this scenario
why: The Scrum Guide specifies that Sprint Review is time-boxed to a maximum of four hours for a one-month Sprint. Each team conducting a two-week Sprint would have a shorter review, but there is no prescribed combined limit.

q: During the Daily Scrum, what happens if a Scrum Team completes their discussion before 15 minutes?
options: They must wait until 15 minutes have passed
They end the Daily Scrum early
They add more agenda items
They postpone it to the next day
why: The Daily Scrum is time-boxed to 15 minutes maximum, not a minimum. If the Developers finish inspecting progress and planning their day in less time, the Daily Scrum ends early.

q: The Daily Scrum is held at the same time and place each day primarily to:
options: Reduce the total time spent in meetings
Reduce complexity and ensure consistency
Impress stakeholders with discipline
Give the Scrum Master a structured role
why: Holding it at the same time and place each day reduces complexity and helps the Developers build a sustainable habit of daily inspection and adaptation.

q: Who is responsible for running the Daily Scrum event?
options: The Scrum Master directs the discussion
The Product Owner sets the agenda
The Developers are responsible for their own Daily Scrum
A rotating Developer leads it
why: The Daily Scrum is owned by the Developers. They run it and determine how they conduct it. The SM ensures it happens and stays within the time-box, but does not direct it.

q: For a two-week Sprint, the maximum time-box for the Sprint Retrospective is:
options: 1.5 hours
2 hours
3 hours
There is no maximum for shorter Sprints
why: Sprint Retrospective is time-boxed to three hours for a one-month Sprint. For a two-week Sprint, it would be scaled proportionally — approximately 1.5 hours.

q: The Sprint Review and the Sprint Retrospective can be combined into one event to save time.
options: True
False
why: False. The Sprint Review (inspect the Increment with stakeholders) and the Sprint Retrospective (inspect the team's process) serve different purposes and should not be combined.

q: What is the primary purpose of the Sprint as a time-boxed event?
options: To measure team velocity
To release the product every month
To create a rhythm for planning, delivery, and inspection in a predictable cadence
To satisfy management reporting requirements
why: The Sprint creates a regular, predictable rhythm for the team to plan, work, inspect, and adapt. This enables sustainable pace and reduces complexity.

q: A Scrum Team plans a two-week Sprint. When does the next Sprint begin?
options: After a one-week planning period
Immediately when the current Sprint ends
After management approves the previous Increment
Only after a release to production
why: A new Sprint starts immediately after the previous one ends. There is no gap between Sprints. The rhythm is continuous.

q: Which Scrum event directly addresses continuous improvement?
options: Sprint Planning
Daily Scrum
Sprint Review
Sprint Retrospective
why: The Sprint Retrospective is where the team inspects itself and identifies ways to improve regarding people, interactions, processes, tools, and the Definition of Done.

q: During the Sprint Review, the stakeholder says the Increment doesn't meet expectations. What is the appropriate action?
options: The Developers immediately rework it to fix the issue
The feedback is discussed and informs future Product Backlog ordering and refinement
The Product Owner must cancel the Sprint
The item is marked as Done anyway
why: The Sprint Review is a working session for collaboration and learning. Stakeholder feedback informs product direction. The item is already Done; future work may adjust based on feedback.

q: A Scrum Team's Daily Scrum conversations repeatedly run over time and get pulled into detailed problem-solving. What should the Scrum Master do?
options: Cancel the Daily Scrum to save time
Coach the team that the Daily Scrum is for inspection and planning, not deep problem-solving; detailed discussions happen outside the event
Require shorter status updates
Extend the time-box to 30 minutes
why: The Daily Scrum is a 15-minute time-boxed inspection. Detailed problem-solving should happen in separate discussions outside the Daily Scrum. The SM coaches the team on this distinction.

q: What does the Product Backlog primarily represent?
options: A list of work completed in past Sprints
An emergent, ordered list of work needed to improve the product toward the Product Goal
The tasks assigned to individual Developers
A contract with customers
why: The Product Backlog is an emergent, ordered list of what is needed to improve the product. It is the single source of work for the Scrum Team and evolves over time.

q: The Product Backlog is static; once items are added, their order rarely changes.
options: True
False
why: False. The Product Backlog is emergent and dynamic. The Product Owner continuously refines, reorders, and adds/removes items based on feedback and learning.

q: Who can modify the Sprint Backlog during a Sprint?
options: Only the Product Owner
Only the Scrum Master
The Developers, as they learn and work toward the Sprint Goal
No one — it is locked once the Sprint begins
why: The Sprint Backlog is owned and managed by the Developers. They update it continuously as work progresses and more is learned, always aiming for the Sprint Goal.

q: An Increment is best described as:
options: A partial, unfinished piece of work
A concrete step toward the Product Goal that is usable and meets the Definition of Done
Everything the team did in a Sprint, finished or not
A release to production
why: An Increment is a concrete, fully verified stepping stone toward the Product Goal. It must meet the Definition of Done and can be demonstrated at the Sprint Review.

q: If an organization has no existing Definition of Done, who creates one?
options: The Scrum Master creates it and enforces it
The Product Owner defines it
The Scrum Team creates one appropriate for their product
Upper management defines it for all teams
why: If the organization has no Definition of Done standard, the Scrum Team creates one appropriate for the product they are building.

q: Why is transparency of the Product Backlog critical?
options: It helps the Scrum Master manage the team
It allows everyone to understand what work is prioritized and why, enabling alignment and reducing surprises
It guarantees faster delivery
It eliminates the need for the Sprint Review
why: Transparency of the Product Backlog enables the Developers, stakeholders, and organization to understand what is planned, why, and in what order. It creates a shared understanding.

q: The Definition of Done can be different for different Product Backlog items within the same Scrum Team.
options: True
False
why: False. The Definition of Done is a shared standard that applies to all Product Backlog items. It ensures consistency and transparency.

q: Which of the following is NOT a part of the Sprint Backlog?
options: The Sprint Goal
Selected Product Backlog items
The actionable plan for delivering the Increment
A list of all completed items from prior Sprints
why: The Sprint Backlog consists of the Sprint Goal, the Product Backlog items selected for this Sprint, and the action plan. Prior Sprints' work is not part of the current Sprint Backlog.

q: The Sprint Goal is primarily committed to by:
options: The Product Owner
The Scrum Master
The Developers
The stakeholders
why: The Developers commit to the Sprint Goal. They created it in Sprint Planning with the Product Owner and they are accountable for achieving it.

q: If the Developers realize mid-Sprint that the Sprint Goal is unachievable due to unexpected complexity, they should:
options: Work overtime to achieve it
Reduce quality to meet the deadline
Collaborate with the Product Owner to re-negotiate scope while protecting the Sprint Goal where possible
Cancel the Sprint
why: The Sprint Goal can be protected even as scope adjusts. The Developers work with the PO to descope lower-priority items while keeping the core Sprint Goal intact.

q: The Product Goal serves as a commitment for which artifact?
options: The Sprint Backlog
The Increment
The Product Backlog
The Definition of Done
why: The Product Goal is the commitment for the Product Backlog. It describes the future state the team is working toward over multiple Sprints.

q: The Definition of Done is a commitment made by the Developers to quality standards.
options: True
False
why: True. The Definition of Done is the commitment for the Increment. It ensures that when an item is marked Done, it truly meets the team's quality and completeness standards.

q: The Developers want to refactor significant portions of the codebase mid-Sprint, which threatens the Sprint Goal. What should happen?
options: They refactor without discussion to improve quality
The Scrum Master forbids it
The team discusses the risk, decides together whether refactoring aligns with the Sprint Goal, and only proceeds if it does
The Product Owner assigns the refactoring to a different Sprint
why: Self-managing Developers make technical decisions together. Refactoring mid-Sprint is legitimate if it serves the Sprint Goal and the team agrees it is the right call.

q: The Scrum Master notices that backlog refinement is not happening regularly. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Shift refinement to a formal scheduled event
Coach the Product Owner and team on the importance of continuous refinement to keep items ready for Sprint Planning
Cancel the next Sprint until refinement is complete
Take over refinement personally
why: Refinement is an ongoing activity (not a formal event). The SM coaches the team on best practices for continuous refinement so items are ready for selection.

q: Which statement best describes accountability within a Scrum Team?
options: The Scrum Master is accountable for team performance
The Product Owner is accountable for completion of Sprint goals
The whole Scrum Team is accountable for creating a valuable, useful Increment every Sprint
Individual Developers are accountable for their assigned tasks
why: The Scrum Guide 2020 states that the whole Scrum Team is accountable for creating a valuable, useful Increment every Sprint. This is collective accountability, not distributed.

q: A Scrum Team must have at least one member who is a project manager.
options: True
False
why: False. Scrum defines the Product Owner, Developers, and Scrum Master — not a project manager. If an organization has PMs, they work outside the Scrum Team or leave the role to fulfill a Scrum role.

q: A Scrum Team of 12 people should:
options: Divide into sub-teams with separate Product Backlogs
Restructure into multiple Scrum Teams sharing the same Product Goal and Product Backlog
Add another Scrum Master to manage half the team
Remain as one team if members prefer it
why: Scrum Teams are typically 10 or fewer. If a team exceeds this, they should reorganize into multiple cohesive Scrum Teams sharing the same Product Goal and Product Backlog.

q: The Product Owner participates in the Daily Scrum primarily to:
options: Report daily metrics to management
Only if they are actively working on a Sprint Backlog item as a Developer
Clarify requirements for the team
Approve each task before Developers begin
why: The Daily Scrum is for the Developers. The PO or SM participates as Developers only if actively working on Sprint Backlog items. They don't attend to manage or approve.

q: Which of the following are true about the Scrum Team structure? (Select all that apply)
options: There are no sub-teams or hierarchies within a Scrum Team
The team is self-managing in who does what, when, and how
The team shares one Product Goal
Members must stay with the same team indefinitely
The team has all skills needed to create value each Sprint without relying on others outside the team
why: A Scrum Team is one cohesive unit with no sub-teams or hierarchies, self-managing, focused on one Product Goal, and cross-functional. Members may transition between teams; tenure is not fixed.

q: When a Scrum Team member consistently fails to meet commitments, the Scrum Master should:
options: Publicly criticize them in the next Daily Scrum
Coach the individual and the team on self-management, transparency, and accountability
Recommend their removal from the team
Stop including them in Sprint Planning
why: The SM serves by coaching the team on self-management and holding each other accountable. Removing someone or excluding them from planning bypasses the coaching opportunity.

q: A Scrum Team may assign different Sprint Goals to different members based on their specialties.
options: True
False
why: False. The Sprint Goal is one single objective for the entire Scrum Team. It creates coherence and focus. The team works together toward it, not toward individual goals.

q: What does it mean for a Scrum Team to be 'focused'?
options: Members concentrate intensely without breaks
The team works on one Product Goal at a time, committed to the Sprint Goal
Developers focus only on coding, PO on requirements, SM on facilitation
The team ignores interruptions entirely
why: Focus in Scrum means commitment to one Product Goal and one Sprint Goal at a time. This creates clarity and coherence — not fragmented work across competing objectives.

q: Which Scrum Value is most directly related to accepting feedback and adjusting plans as new information emerges?
options: Commitment
Respect
Openness
Focus
why: Openness to new information and perspectives enables inspection and adaptation. Empiricism depends on the willingness to hear feedback and adjust course.

q: A Scrum Team discovers a critical quality issue in production after the Sprint Review. Which value is most important to embody in responding?
options: Focus — ignore the problem and stay on the Sprint plan
Courage — raise the issue transparently and address it, even if it disrupts the plan
Commitment — promise to fix it later
Respect — let management decide the response
why: Courage paired with openness means the team transparently raises issues and acts decisively, even when it's uncomfortable. Hiding problems or deferring defeats empiricism.

q: Empiricism requires that the Scrum Team predict exactly what will happen in the next Sprint.
options: True
False
why: False. Empiricism is about learning from evidence and adapting based on what is observed. Forecasts evolve as more is learned; exact prediction is neither possible nor required.

q: Why is transparency of the Definition of Done particularly critical in Scrum?
options: So Developers know when to stop working
So the Product Owner can enforce quality standards
Without a shared, transparent DoD, the team cannot reliably assess what is truly done and make sound decisions
To impress stakeholders with quality
why: Transparency of the DoD allows the team to inspect whether work actually meets the commitment. Without it, hidden defects accumulate and decisions are made on incomplete information.

q: A team member says 'We've always done it this way.' How does empiricism respond to this claim?
options: Accept it — tradition is reliable
Ignore it — new methods are always better
Inspect whether it still serves the team's goals; if evidence shows a better way, adapt
Ask management to decide
why: Empiricism requires inspection and adaptation. 'We've always done it' is not evidence. The team inspects results and adapts practices based on what works now.

q: Which of the following support transparency in a Scrum Team? (Select all that apply)
options: A visible, updated Sprint Backlog showing progress toward the Sprint Goal
Regular, honest Daily Scrums where Developers talk about impediments
An evolving Definition of Done that reflects quality standards
Hiding known issues until the Sprint Review to avoid worry
Clear Product Backlog ordering that the team understands
why: Transparency comes from visible artifacts (Sprint Backlog, Product Backlog), honest communication (Daily Scrums), and a shared Definition of Done. Hiding issues destroys transparency.

q: A Developer says, 'I finished my task, but I haven't integrated it yet.' The most Scrum-aligned response is:
options: Great, you're done for the day
It's only done when it meets the Definition of Done — work is not complete until integrated and tested
Integration is the Product Owner's responsibility
Done means deployed to production
why: Done means work meets the Definition of Done. Typically that includes integration, testing, and ready for release. 'Finished my part' is not the same as Done.

q: The Scrum Team discovers mid-Sprint that they misunderstood a critical requirement. The Scrum Master should facilitate:
options: Continuing with the misunderstanding to avoid disruption
A conversation with the Product Owner to clarify and renegotiate scope within the Sprint Goal if needed
Cancelling the Sprint immediately
Asking Developers to work extra hours to fix the misunderstanding
why: Inspection and adaptation are core. The SM helps the team and PO clarify, then adjust the Sprint Backlog. Pushing forward with wrong understanding wastes value.

q: Management wants the Scrum Team to adopt a mandatory hand-off process for code reviews. The Scrum Master should:
options: Implement it immediately to keep management happy
Ban the process to protect the team's autonomy
Coach both the team and management on self-management and cross-functionality; discuss whether the process serves quality and the Sprint Goal
Let the Developers vote on whether to accept it
why: The SM serves both the team and the organization. Imposed processes may undermine self-management or efficiency. The SM coaches on empirical evidence and helps find a path forward.

q: A talented Developer says they don't like Scrum ceremonies and wants to skip the Daily Scrum. How should the Scrum Master respond?
options: Let them skip — talented individuals deserve flexibility
Make attendance mandatory with no discussion
Coach the Developer on the purpose of the Daily Scrum (inspection and adaptation); if concerns persist, address root causes (meeting format, timing, etc.)
Move the Daily Scrum to a time that works only for others
why: The SM coaches on Scrum fundamentals and listens to concerns. If a ceremony isn't working, the SM facilitates improvement — adjusting format, time, or approach — rather than forcing or enabling avoidance.

q: The organization operates in a matrix structure where Developers report to functional managers. The Scrum Master should:
options: Avoid the matrix conflict — it's not the SM's concern
Help negotiate clarity: Scrum Team members report to the SM for Scrum work; functional managers may manage career paths outside of Scrum responsibilities
Tell functional managers their authority is invalid
Recommend the organization eliminate all managers
why: Serving the organization includes helping it adapt structures to Scrum. The SM negotiates clarity on authority and reporting so the team can self-manage Scrum work.

q: A Product Owner has not participated in refinement and arrives at Sprint Planning unprepared. The Scrum Master should:
options: Cancel the Sprint Planning and reschedule
Have the Developers select items without PO input
Coach the PO on the importance of refinement and readiness; postpone Planning if needed to protect quality
Skip Sprint Planning this Sprint
why: Effective planning requires a prepared PO. The SM coaches on the process and protects the team from making uninformed selections. Skipping or working unprepared undermines empiricism.

q: During the Sprint, a business crisis occurs and an executive demands the team drop current work to handle it. The Scrum Master should:
options: Tell the team to ignore the executive
Immediately shift all team members to the crisis
Help the executive, the Product Owner, and the team understand the consequences and work through the decision empirically
Tell the executive that Scrum doesn't allow interruptions
why: The SM serves the organization and the team. Urgent requests happen. The SM facilitates transparent discussion of trade-offs: What gets dropped? What's the real impact on the Sprint Goal? This is the PO's call with full information.

q: The Developers want to refactor legacy code, but the Product Owner sees no user-facing value. The Scrum Master should:
options: Side with the PO — refactoring is not valuable
Side with the Developers — technical work is always important
Facilitate a conversation about quality, speed, and the Product Goal; help both sides see trade-offs and make an informed choice
Split the Sprint between refactoring and features
why: This is a genuine tension between quality and throughput. The SM doesn't referee with authority; they coach both sides to understand that technical debt slows future value and refactoring serves the Product Goal.

q: A Scrum Team has been stable for two years. Team members suggest removing some ceremonies to 'move faster.' The Scrum Master should:
options: Approve — experienced teams need less overhead
Refuse — Scrum requires all events
Explore the concern empirically: Why do they feel ceremonies slow them down? Inspect how events are run; adapt the format or time-boxing, but don't skip events
Compromise by reducing the Daily Scrum to 10 minutes
why: Events are not optional, but their execution can improve. If ceremonies feel inefficient, the SM helps the team inspect and adapt the format — not eliminate them.

q: The Sprint Review is scheduled but no stakeholders have confirmed attendance. The Scrum Master should:
options: Cancel the Sprint Review
Hold the Sprint Review anyway with only the team present
Work to secure stakeholder attendance; if not possible, reschedule to ensure collaboration happens
Have the Scrum Master represent stakeholder feedback
why: The Sprint Review is a working session for the Scrum Team to inspect the Increment with stakeholders. Without them, critical feedback is lost. The SM ensures the event serves its purpose.

q: A retrospective reveals that the team feels disrespected by management's attitude toward their work. The Scrum Master should:
options: Tell the team to ignore management's attitude
Coach the team to be more resilient
Facilitate the team identifying an improvement action, then serve as a bridge: coach the organization on respect and psychological safety
Cancel the next retrospective
why: Respect is a Scrum Value. If the team names this as an impediment, the SM coaches both the team and the organization to address root causes of disrespect.

q: Two Developers strongly disagree on whether work meets the Definition of Done. How should this be resolved?
options: The Scrum Master decides
The Product Owner decides
The Developers, as self-managing professionals, resolve it together; if needed, clarify the DoD with the whole team
Escalate to a quality assurance department
why: Self-management means the Developers collectively resolve internal disagreements on quality and process. If the DoD is ambiguous, the team clarifies it together.

q: A Developer keeps adding features beyond the scope of the Sprint Backlog item without discussing it. The Scrum Master should:
options: Praise them for going above and beyond
Tell them to stop adding scope
Coach the Developer and team on transparent collaboration: discuss scope changes with the PO and team, prevent hidden work that surprises
Report them to management
why: Scope creep undermines transparency and planning. The SM coaches on collaboration: if work changes, the team and PO know. The SM also helps the team understand the impact on focus and the Sprint Goal.

q: The Product Owner and a major stakeholder disagree on priority. The Scrum Master should:
options: Take a vote
Coach both to reach consensus, but ultimately support the Product Owner's authority to order the Product Backlog
Delay the decision until the next retrospective
Override the PO and follow the stakeholder's preference
why: The PO is accountable for Product Backlog ordering and maximizing value. The SM helps the PO engage with stakeholders and hear their input, but the final call is the PO's.

q: During Sprint Planning, the Developers commit to more work than their historical capacity suggests they can handle. The Scrum Master should:
options: Force them to reduce the commitment
Let them commit; they learn by failing
Facilitate a conversation: share historical data, discuss confidence level, and coach on self-awareness and realistic forecasting
Skip Sprint Planning if capacity is uncertain
why: The SM coaches the team on data-driven forecasting and self-management. Sharing past data helps them make realistic commitments; the team decides, not the SM.

q: A Scrum Team's Increment is complete but the business isn't ready to release it. The Scrum Master should:
options: Hold the Increment in a pending state with no review
Encourage the team to keep working on the same Backlog items
Coach the team that the Increment is done; work with the organization on release timing and the product roadmap
Tell the team not to mark items as Done
why: A Done Increment is a Done Increment. Release timing is a business decision separate from Scrum. The SM helps the organization understand that Done doesn't always mean released immediately.

q: The Scrum Team works on a safety-critical system. Who is ultimately accountable for quality and completeness of the Increment?
options: The quality assurance department
The Scrum Master
The whole Scrum Team, supported by their Definition of Done and any organizational standards
Senior management
why: The Scrum Team is accountable for creating a valuable, useful Increment every Sprint. In high-risk domains, the DoD may be more stringent, but accountability remains with the team.

q: A Scrum Team member is physically co-located with the team but emotionally withdrawn and doesn't speak in meetings. The Scrum Master should:
options: Force them to speak up
Leave them alone
Coach the team on psychological safety; individually support the team member; create space for quiet voices
Recommend their removal
why: Respect and courage are Scrum Values. The SM creates an environment where all voices are valued. This may involve one-on-ones, adjusting formats, or coaching the team on inclusion.

q: The Product Owner and Scrum Master are the same person. This arrangement:
options: Is ideal — fewer people, less overhead
Is forbidden by Scrum
Can work if the person maintains clear separation of the two roles; however, serving both is challenging and creates potential conflicts of interest
Should only be done in very small organizations
why: Scrum doesn't forbid one person holding two roles, but the accountabilities are distinct and often pull in different directions. It's possible but challenging.

q: A Scrum Team is distributed across three time zones. Daily Scrum attendance is low because of scheduling conflicts. The Scrum Master should:
options: Cancel the Daily Scrum
Make attendance optional
Facilitate the team finding a time that includes everyone, or explore asynchronous formats that preserve the purpose of inspection and adaptation
Demand everyone join at the earliest time zone
why: The Daily Scrum's purpose is inspection and adaptation. The SM helps the team find a format and timing that enables that purpose — whether synchronous or asynchronous.

q: A Developer leaves the team mid-Sprint. The remaining Developers say they can absorb the work. The Scrum Master should:
options: Approve the change and monitor closely
Cancel the Sprint
Facilitate the team assessing the impact: Can the Sprint Goal still be met? Should Sprint Backlog be renegotiated with the PO? What support is needed?
Hire a replacement immediately
why: The SM helps the team inspect and adapt to the change. The team decides whether to press on, adjust scope, or escalate. Self-management means the team owns the decision with good information.

q: The organization is considering moving to a predictive, waterfall approach for a new project. The Scrum Master should:
options: Insist Scrum is mandatory
Support Waterfall — Scrum isn't right for all projects
Coach the organization on empiricism and Scrum; help them understand when Scrum is a good fit and when other approaches may be better
Resign
why: The SM serves the organization by training and coaching on Scrum and empirical product development. This includes helping the org understand context and choosing wisely.

q: Developers are burned out and working weekends to keep up. The Scrum Master should:
options: Praise their dedication
Tell them to stop and rest
Identify systemic causes (unrealistic forecasting, scope creep, technical debt) and coach the team and organization to address root causes
Reduce the Sprint length to make weeks shorter
why: Burnout signals an impediment. The SM coaches empirically: What's the root cause? Unrealistic expectations? Lack of skills? Technical debt slowing progress? Address the cause, not just the symptom.

q: A Scrum Team's Retrospective action items are never completed. The Scrum Master should:
options: Stop holding retrospectives
Make action items optional
Coach the team on prioritizing improvements; help identify which action is most impactful and commit to it in the next Sprint
Assign action items to individuals for accountability
why: Continuous improvement requires follow-through. The SM helps the team prioritize and own improvements. Often, one high-impact action in the Sprint Backlog is better than many half-done ones.

q: The Scrum Team disagrees with the organizational Definition of Done and wants to lower standards. The Scrum Master should:
options: Let them — team ownership is key
Enforce the organizational standard; no changes allowed
Facilitate a conversation: understand why the team wants to lower it, discuss quality impacts and technical debt, and help them see the full picture
Create a separate DoD for this team only
why: If the organization has a DoD standard, the team must follow it as a minimum. However, the SM coaches on why it exists and explores whether the concern signals a real problem (unclear DoD, unrealistic expectations, skill gaps).

q: A Scrum Master is coaching a team new to Scrum. What is the primary message about self-management?
options: Do whatever you want; there are no rules
The team decides who does what, when, and how — within the framework of Scrum and the Sprint Goal
The Scrum Master will tell you what to do
Self-management means you never need guidance
why: Self-managing doesn't mean 'no guidance' or 'chaos.' The team owns how it works within the Scrum framework, Sprint commitments, and organizational context.
